title: Understanding Back Stiffness and Reduced Grooming in Cats
meta_desc: Is your cat stiff and stopped grooming? Learn about arthritis, mobility issues, and when to see a vet for your cat's spinal discomfort.
content_html:
What is Decreased Grooming Due to Back Stiffness?

When a cat stops grooming their back or hindquarters, it is often a sign of physical discomfort or a loss of flexibility. This is frequently caused by Osteoarthritis or Degenerative Joint Disease (DJD). Because cats are naturally very flexible, a noticeable inability to reach their lower back, resulting in mats or dandruff, suggests that the spine or hips are too stiff or painful to bend normally.

Is it an emergency?

Urgency Level: Low. In most cases, stiffness and reduced grooming are chronic conditions that develop over time rather than acute emergencies. While your cat is likely experiencing some level of discomfort, it does not require an emergency room visit unless it is accompanied by a total loss of hind limb function, inability to urinate, or extreme vocalization. You should, however, schedule a routine veterinary appointment within the week to discuss pain management.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Providing your veterinarian with a photo of your cat's coat can show the exact distribution of mats or unkempt fur, which helps pinpoint the area of stiffness. Even more helpful is a video of your cat walking, jumping, or trying to groom. These visuals allow the vet to see your cat's natural movement in a stress-free environment, as many cats hide their stiffness or limping when they are nervous at the clinic.

faqs.question: Why is my cat's fur matting on their lower back?
faqs.answer: Matting on the lower back often occurs because the cat can no longer reach that area due to spinal stiffness or arthritis pain.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human pain relief for stiffness?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your cat human medications like ibuprofen or acetaminophen, as these are highly toxic to cats. Only use medications prescribed by your vet.

faqs.question: How can I help my stiff cat feel more comfortable?
faqs.answer: Provide low-sided litter boxes, soft bedding in warm areas, and use a soft brush to help them groom the hard-to-reach spots on their back.
